claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 21 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 11 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by setting up your assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started with your Child Benefit eligibility assessment, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

The age of your child is important because:
- Children under 16 may automatically qualify
- Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and wanted to check if I'm eligible. I actually have three children I'd like to ask about - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Alex is 21, Blake is 15, and Charlie is 11 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! Let me help you check eligibility for all three of your children: Alex (21), Blake (15), and Charlie (11).

Let me work through each child based on their age:

**For Charlie (11 years old) - Under 16:**
Charlie is under 16, so let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Charlie (11 years old):**
Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

**For Blake (15 years old):**
Blake is also under 16, so I'll need to ask the same question about Blake.

**For Alex (21 years old):**
Unfortunately, Alex at 21 years old is over the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit generally only covers children up to age 16, or up to age 19 if they're in approved education or training. At age 21, Alex would not qualify for Child Benefit.

Let me start with Charlie and Blake - are either of them working 24 hours or more per week? (I'm assuming Charlie at 11 is not working, but please confirm for both children.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Charlie doesn't work at all - he's only 11! Blake doesn't work either, or just maybe does a little pocket money job on the weekends, nothing serious.

But about Alex - he does work full-time actually. Does that definitely mean he's not eligible then?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! Let me address each child:

**Charlie (11 years old):** Not working - ✓
**Blake (15 years old):** Not working full-time (just a small weekend job) - ✓

**About Alex (21 years old):** Yes, I'm afraid Alex is not eligible for Child Benefit. The age limit is firm - Child Benefit only covers:
- Children under 16, OR
- Young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training

Since Alex is 21, he's over the maximum age limit regardless of his work situation or education status. This means you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.

However, let me continue checking eligibility for Charlie and Blake, as they may both qualify.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Charlie and Blake:**

Are Charlie and Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(This would be benefits claimed by them personally, not benefits you receive for them.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own names. They're just kids living at home - well, actually, Charlie lives with me but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that important information. Let me continue checking the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Charlie and Blake:**

Are either Charlie or Blake enrolled in an apprenticeship program? 

(Most apprenticeships disqualify from Child Benefit, except for Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales which are approved.)

Also, you mentioned that **Blake doesn't live with you** - this is important. I'll need to ask you more about this situation:

- Where does Blake live? 
- Do you financially support Blake or contribute to Blake's upbringing?
- Does someone else live with Blake who might also be claiming Child Benefit for them?

These questions matter because to claim Child Benefit, you generally need to be responsible for the child, even if they don't live with you full-time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Neither Charlie nor Blake are doing any apprenticeships - they're too young for that really.

About Blake - he doesn't live with me, he lives elsewhere. I do help out a bit financially when I can, maybe about £10 a week for bits and pieces. Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake as far as I know.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Neither are in apprenticeships, so that's fine. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to ask about your residency:**

Do you normally live in the UK? This is one of the key requirements for claiming Child Benefit.

(If you don't normally live in the UK, there are some special circumstances that might still qualify you, such as having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or being a Crown servant abroad.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in the UK satisfies the residency requirement. Now let me check about your responsibility for the children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: This is an important point. Let me get some information about the Child Benefit payment amount to help determine responsibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, this is crucial for understanding your eligibility for each child:

**For Charlie (11, lives with you):** Since Charlie lives with you, you meet the responsibility requirement. ✓

**For Blake (15, doesn't live with you):** You mentioned you contribute about £10 a week for "bits and pieces." To be considered responsible for Blake, you need to contribute at least as much as the Child Benefit payment amount towards their care.

Current Child Benefit rates are:
- **Eldest or only child: £25.60 per week**
- **Additional children: £16.95 per week each**

Your £10 per week contribution is below the Child Benefit rate. To claim Child Benefit for Blake, you would need to show you're contributing **at least £25.60 per week** (if Blake is your only claim) or **£16.95 per week** (if claiming for multiple children) towards Blake's upkeep. This can include money for food, clothes, pocket money, housing costs, or presents.

**Questions:**
1. If you also count occasional presents or other support, would your total contribution to Blake reach at least £16.95 per week on average?
2. Who does Blake live with? Is that person responsible for Blake's day-to-day care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, I see. No, if I'm honest it's really just about £10 a week - I help when I can but it doesn't add up to that much. Blake lives with their other parent who looks after them day-to-day.

So does that mean I can't claim for Blake then?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about the situation. Yes, unfortunately, based on what you've told me, **you would not be able to claim Child Benefit for Blake** because you don't meet the responsibility requirement. 

To claim Child Benefit, you need to either:
- Live with the child, OR
- Contribute at least as much as the Child Benefit rate (£25.60/week for an only child, or £16.95/week for additional children) towards their upkeep

Since Blake lives with their other parent who provides the day-to-day care, and your contribution is around £10 per week (which is below the threshold), you don't meet the responsibility criteria for Blake.

**However, Blake's other parent could claim Child Benefit for Blake** if they meet all the eligibility requirements.

**For Charlie (11 years old),** let me continue checking your eligibility since Charlie lives with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Charlie:**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Charlie? (For example, Charlie's other parent or another family member?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's clear. Let me continue with Charlie's eligibility check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Charlie's living situation:**

Is Charlie currently:
- In hospital (as an inpatient)?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or living normally with you at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie is living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue checking the requirements for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question about Charlie:**

Is the local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance? 

(This would be things like the council paying for Charlie's housing or upkeep costs.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Charlie - I'm looking after him myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me get the final outcome for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me provide you with the complete eligibility assessment for all three of your children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results**

I've completed the assessment for all three of your children. Here's the outcome:

---

### **Alex (21 years old) - ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

**Reason:** Alex is over the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit only covers:
- Children under 16, OR
- Young people aged 16-19 in approved education or training

Since Alex is 21, they don't qualify regardless of their work status or any other circumstances.

---

### **Blake (15 years old) - ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

**Reason:** While Blake meets the age requirement, you don't meet the **responsibility requirement** for Blake. 

Blake lives with their other parent who provides day-to-day care, and your financial contribution of approximately £10 per week falls below the minimum threshold. To claim Child Benefit for a child who doesn't live with you, you need to contribute at least **£16.95 per week** towards their upkeep (this is the Child Benefit rate for additional children).

**Important:** Blake's other parent may be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake if they meet all the requirements.

---

### **Charlie (11 years old) - ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Great news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie because:
- Charlie is under 16 ✓
- Charlie lives with you ✓
- You live in the UK ✓
- No one else is claiming for Charlie ✓
- The council isn't paying for Charlie's care ✓

---

## **Next Steps for Charlie:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit:**
   - Apply online at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim
   - Or by post using form CH2

2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Charlie's birth certificate or adoption certificate

3. **Payments:**
   - You'll receive **£25.60 per week** for Charlie (as your only eligible child)
   - Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

4. **Important Note - High Income Child Benefit Charge:**
   - If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the tax system
   - This doesn't affect your eligibility, but it's something to be aware of

---

**Do you have any questions about these results or need help with anything else?**

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old, which exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit only covers children under 16, or young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Since Alex is over 20, they do not qualify regardless of work status or other circumstances.

Blake is 15 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). Blake is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, Blake does not live with the claimant and the claimant only contributes approximately £10 per week towards Blake's upkeep. To meet the responsibility requirement, the claimant would need to contribute at least £16.95 per week (the Child Benefit rate for additional children) towards Blake's care. Since the contribution falls below this threshold and Blake lives with their other parent who provides day-to-day care, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement for Blake.

Charlie is 11 years old (under 16), does not work, is not receiving benefits in their own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), satisfying the residency requirement. Charlie lives with the claimant, which satisfies the responsibility requirement. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered), and the local council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance. Charlie meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children but can only claim Child Benefit for one of them (Charlie). Alex is ineligible due to being over the age limit (21 years old). Blake is ineligible because the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement - Blake lives with their other parent and the claimant's financial contribution (£10/week) is below the required threshold (£16.95/week minimum). Charlie meets all eligibility requirements as they are under 16, live with the claimant, and no disqualifying factors apply.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 21 years old, which exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit only covers children under 16, or young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Since Alex is over 20, they do not qualify regardless of work status or other circumstances.

Blake is 15 years old and meets the age requirement (under 16). Blake is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, Blake does not live with the claimant and the claimant only contributes approximately £10 per week towards Blake's upkeep. To meet the responsibility requirement, the claimant would need to contribute at least £16.95 per week (the Child Benefit rate for additional children) towards Blake's care. Since the contribution falls below this threshold and Blake lives with their other parent who provides day-to-day care, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement for Blake.

Charlie is 11 years old (under 16), does not work, is not receiving benefits in their own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), satisfying the residency requirement. Charlie lives with the claimant, which satisfies the responsibility requirement. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Charlie is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered), and the local council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance. Charlie meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children but can only claim Child Benefit for one of them (Charlie). Alex is ineligible due to being over the age limit (21 years old). Blake is ineligible because the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement - Blake lives with their other parent and the claimant's financial contribution (£10/week) is below the required threshold (£16.95/week minimum). Charlie meets all eligibility requirements as they are under 16, live with the claimant, and no disqualifying factors apply.
